name: Social Security: Replacement Card, Benefit Estimate, or Direct-Deposit Change
description: Call the SSA national line or a local field office to request a replacement Social Security card, get a benefit estimate, or change direct-deposit details on an active benefit. Most card replacements are now done via my Social Security online — this skill is for cases where the online flow fails or in-person ID is required.
disclaimer: Not legal or financial advice. The agent will not advise on when to start benefits, spousal-claiming strategy, or disability-determination strategy — those require an SSA claims rep, a financial planner, or an attorney specialising in SSA matters.

context.when_to_use: User cannot complete the request through my Social Security online (account locked, ID.me/Login.gov rejection, no online account possible due to credit-freeze conflict). User needs an in-person appointment confirmed. User received SSA correspondence and wants to confirm authenticity. User wants to change banking info on an active benefit.
preconditions: User has their Social Security Number (will only be read aloud after rep authenticates).
User has full legal name, date of birth, place of birth, and mother's maiden name.
User has current address on file and any prior address (for KBA questions).
For direct-deposit changes: new bank routing number, account number, and account type.
Patience: SSA wait times average 30–60 minutes on the 1-800-772-1213 line.

principles: SSA is one of the most ID-strict federal agencies. Direct-deposit changes especially are a fraud target — expect rigorous authentication and possibly a mailed confirmation that delays the change.

The national 800 number gets you a general claims rep. Local field offices get you someone who can pull your file faster — but the field-office line is harder to reach. Try the national line first; if escalation is needed, ask for the local office direct number.
Best call times: Wednesday–Friday after 4pm Eastern, or first thing Tuesday–Friday morning. Avoid Monday and the first week of any month (peak benefit-payment questions).
If the user is a representative payee, that triggers different authentication and forms — establish payee status up front.
SSA will NOT threaten arrest, demand gift cards, or call out of the blue saying your SSN is suspended. Any such contact is a scam.

phrases.opener_card: Hi, I'd like to request a replacement Social Security card. The online process [didn't work / isn't available for me] because [reason]. Can we start that by phone, or do I need an in-person appointment?
phrases.opener_estimate: Hi, I'd like a benefit estimate for retirement at ages 62, 67, and 70. Can you pull that and read me the figures?
phrases.opener_dd_change: Hi, I'd like to change the direct-deposit account on my Social Security benefit. The new bank is [BANK], routing [ROUTING], account [LAST 4 ONLY until authenticated]. Can we do that today?
phrases.ask_for_field_office: Could I have the direct phone number for my local field office? I think this might need an in-person appointment.
phrases.authentication_ready: I have my SSN, DOB, place of birth, mother's maiden name, and current address ready when you need them.
phrases.stall_documents: One moment — I want to read the numbers off my [bank statement / SSA letter] so I get them right.
phrases.graceful_close: Thank you. Can I confirm: what was changed, when it takes effect, whether I'll receive written confirmation in the mail, and a confirmation or case number — and your name and ID?

tactics.name: Try my Social Security online first if not already done
tactics.script: Card replacement, benefit estimate, and DD change are all online-eligible in most states. The line is busy; the website is fast. Only call if the online flow has actually failed.

tactics.name: Establish account type up front
tactics.script: Tell the rep: retirement / SSDI / SSI / survivors / representative-payee. Different programs route to different specialists.

tactics.name: For DD changes, expect a hold-and-mail
tactics.script: SSA typically requires the change to be made by phone WITH a mailed confirmation that takes 30–60 days, OR via my Social Security with stronger online ID. Tell the user this delay is normal and not failure.

tactics.name: Ask for an in-person appointment if ID is the blocker
tactics.when: Card replacement requires original-document ID inspection.
tactics.script: If the user cannot prove identity online (no driver's license that matches, foreign-document case, name change), the rep can schedule an in-person appointment at the local field office. Get the appointment confirmation number and the list of documents required.

tactics.name: Use SSA-1099 / award letter as backup ID
tactics.when: User has trouble authenticating on the phone.
tactics.script: Reps can sometimes use the most recent SSA-1099 or benefit-award letter as a secondary authenticator. Have it ready.

boundaries: Do NOT advise on the optimal age to claim benefits, spousal/widow(er) strategy, or whether to file for disability — refer to a financial planner or SSA claims rep.
Do NOT share the user's SSN until the rep has been confirmed as SSA and has asked specifically.
Do NOT initiate a DD change without explicit, real-time authorization from the user — banking changes are a top SSA fraud vector.
Do NOT engage with any inbound caller claiming SSA — call back via 1-800-772-1213 or the local field-office published number.
Do NOT claim a card or appointment is set until you have a written or verbal confirmation number.
success_signals: Rep schedules an in-person appointment with a confirmation number.
Card replacement is accepted by phone and mailed within 10–14 business days.
Benefit estimate is read aloud at ages 62, 67, 70 and matches my Social Security online (sanity check).
DD change is accepted with a verbal confirmation number and a mailed confirmation in 30–60 days.
Rep confirms no fraud flag on the account.
failure_signals: Rep cannot authenticate the user after 2 attempts — schedule an in-person appointment with original documents.
DD change request is rejected because account is under fraud monitoring — handle in person.
User is told the change can only be made online but the user cannot pass Login.gov / ID.me — escalate to field-office in-person.
Hold > 90 minutes — call back later in the week or after 4pm Eastern.

exit_strategy.on_success: Capture: change made, effective date, confirmation/case number, agent name + ID. Note whether a mailed confirmation will arrive (and the expected window). Verbally repeat back banking changes digit-by-digit if applicable.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Capture next step: in-person appointment (date, time, office address, documents to bring), alternative online flow, or follow-up call window. Provide the operator the documents list for the in-person visit.
follow_ups: Calendar the in-person appointment with a 24-hour reminder.
Calendar arrival of the replacement card (10–14 business days).
Confirm the first DD-changed payment landed in the new account (next benefit-payment date).
If a mailed confirmation does not arrive within the quoted window, call back with the confirmation number.

required_user_info: Full legal name, DOB, place of birth, mother's maiden name
Current address (and prior address for KBA)
Program type (retirement / SSDI / SSI / survivor / representative-payee)
For DD changes: bank name, routing number, account number, account type (checking/savings)
Whether the user has online my Social Security access
Documents available for in-person ID (DL, passport, birth certificate, naturalization papers)
hang_up_and_call_back_signals: Inbound call claiming SSA — SSA does not call to suspend SSNs, demand payment, or threaten arrest; hang up and call 1-800-772-1213.
Rep insists on full SSN before identifying themselves and the office — request name, office, and ID; if refused, hang up.
User cannot answer authentication questions — gather prior addresses, prior SSA-1099, and retry.
